title: Single-Object vs. Multi-Object
concept: Single-object detection assumes exactly one object is present, whereas multi-object detection locates and classifies a variable number of objects.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What regression task predicts bounding box coordinates?
exercises.explain: Localization is the regression task of predicting spatial coordinates.

cards.title: A Busy Street
cards.body: If you look at an empty street with one car, predicting its box is easy because the output size is fixed. But what if 20 pedestrians and 15 cars suddenly cross the street? How does the model know how many boxes to output?

cards.title: Single vs. Multi-Object
cards.body: Single-Object Detection assumes the image contains exactly one object of interest. Multi-Object Detection handles an unknown, dynamic number of objects, predicting a variable list of labels and boxes.

cards.title: Spotlight vs. Stage Lights
cards.body: Single-object is like a theater spotlight following a solo singer — the light has one target. Multi-object is like the stage lights illuminating an entire choir, dynamically tracking whoever is moving or singing.

cards.title: Face Unlock vs. Group Photo
cards.body: Face unlock on your phone is a single-object task (it only looks for your face). A group photo app that draws boxes around 10 different friends is a multi-object task.

cards.title: Not just multiple classification runs
cards.body: You might think multi-object detection is just running a single-object detector over and over. However, doing so would be extremely slow and wouldn't handle overlapping objects efficiently.

cards.title: Fixed vs. Dynamic Outputs
cards.body: Comparing single-object vs multi-object detection architecture:

exercises.prompt: Why is multi-object detection harder than single-object detection?
exercises.explain: In multi-object detection, the number of objects is variable and unknown beforehand, requiring architectures that can handle dynamic outputs.

options.text: The model doesn't know in advance how many objects are in the image
The model cannot use convolutional layers
The images must always be grayscale
The model only predicts class labels

exercises.prompt: True or False: Single-object detection networks typically output a fixed number of coordinates (e.g., exactly 4 numbers for one box).
exercises.explain: True. Because they assume only one object is present, they always output exactly one set of bounding box coordinates.
Multi-object detection deals with a variable number of objects, outputting anywhere from zero to hundreds of boxes.
exercises.sentence: While single-object models predict a fixed output, multi-object models must handle a ___ number of detections.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario requires a multi-object detection system?
exercises.explain: Parking lot counting requires detecting multiple objects (cars) of variable count, which is a multi-object detection task.

options.text: Predicting the position of a single tumor in a medical scan where only one can exist
Counting and locating all cars in a satellite image of a parking lot
Determining if a crop image contains a weed or a flower

exercises.prompt: You are building a vision system for a retail store to count customers entering through the main doors. During busy hours, several people walk in together. What kind of model do you need?

options.text: Single-object detection, because we can count them one by one as they cross the line.
Multi-object detection, because multiple customers can occupy the frame at the same time and must be tracked individually.
Image classification, because we only need to know if the store is open or closed.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Since multiple people appear in the frame simultaneously, a multi-object detection model is required. Next: let's explore real-world applications of object detection!
